lesson:
The kanji 今 is a lid (亼) clamping down on something below. The ancient idea: a moment captured, contained, pinned to the present. You can't hold onto 今 — by the time you say it, it's already slipping into the past — but the kanji tries anyway, pressing a lid on the instant to freeze it in place.

いま doesn't just mean "now" in the clock-time sense. It stretches. 今行く can mean "I'm going right this second" or "I'll go in a bit" — it's the speaker's psychological now, not a precise timestamp. When your friend texts 今どこ？ (where are you now?), that's tight, immediate. But 今は忙しい (I'm busy now) could mean this hour, this week, this season of your life. Context does all the work.

今 is a compound machine. It bolts onto other time words to anchor them to the present: 今日 (きょう, today), 今週 (こんしゅう, this week), 今月 (こんげつ, this month), 今年 (ことし, this year). Notice 今日 and 今年 have irregular readings — native Japanese words that got kanji slapped on them — while 今週 and 今月 use the Chinese-derived こん. You'll just have to memorize which is which.

Here's a fun piece of linguistic history: 新しい is pronounced wrong. For over a thousand years, the correct reading was あらたしい — from the root あらた (new, fresh), which you still see in 新たに (あらたに, anew). Sometime during the Edo period, people started swapping the syllables — あらたしい became あたらしい — and the "mistake" stuck. Linguists call this metathesis, and it happens in English too ("comfortable" → "comftrable"). So every time you say あたらしい, you're perpetuating a 300-year-old pronunciation error that became the standard.

The kanji 新 is 立 (stand) + 木 (tree) + 斤 (axe). A standing tree being felled with an axe — the fresh-cut surface of wood, raw and new. The smell of cut timber. That sensory vividness is baked into the character.

新しい vs 新た: Both mean "new," but 新しい is everyday spoken Japanese while 新た (あらた) is literary and formal. 新しい靴 (new shoes) vs. 新たな発見 (a new discovery). You'll hear 新しい in conversation. You'll see 新たな in news articles and speeches.

Dictionaryあたらしい
Negativeあたらしくない
Pastあたらしかった
Past negあたらしくなかった
Adverbあたらしく
て-formあたらしくて

なる is one of the most fundamental verbs in the language — the verb of transformation. Things don't just "be" in Japanese the way they do in English. They become. Spring becomes summer. Children become adults. Silence becomes noise. なる is the engine behind all of it.

The particle it takes tells you everything. に marks the result — what something turns into. 医者になる (become a doctor). 大人になる (become an adult). With い-adjectives, you drop the い and add く: 暑くなる (become hot). With な-adjectives and nouns, に: 静かになる (become quiet). This pattern — [result] + になる or くなる — is how Japanese expresses all change of state. Master it and you unlock an enormous amount of the language.

なる vs する: These two are a philosophical pair. なる is change that happens — things become what they become, organically, without a visible hand. する is change that someone makes happen. 部屋がきれいになった (the room became clean — it just happened) vs. 部屋をきれいにした (I made the room clean — I did it). Japanese naturally gravitates toward なる. Where English says "I decided," Japanese often says 〜ことになった — "it became that..." — framing decisions as outcomes rather than acts of will.

Particles: なる takes に — the thing you become, the state you arrive at. に marks the destination of the transformation. 先生になる (become a teacher). With い-adjectives, the adjective itself changes form (〜くなる) so no particle is needed.

Passive になられる: rare and formal. You might hear it in honorific speech — 社長になられた (he became president — said respectfully). に marks what was become.

Causative を／にならせる: making someone become something. を for coercive, に for permissive. 息子を医者にならせる (make my son become a doctor). Uncommon — Japanese more naturally uses 〜にさせる with する instead.

五段 Godan自動詞
Dictionaryなるに
Negativeならないに
Politeなりますに
Polite negなりませんに
Pastなったに
Polite pastなりましたに
て-formなってに
Volitionalなろうに
Polite volなりましょうに
Passiveなられるに
Causativeならせるを／に
Caus-passならせられるに
Potentialなれるに
Imperativeなれに
Imp なさいなりなさいに
Cond ～ばなればに
Cond ～たらなったらに

まだ lives a double life depending on whether the sentence is positive or negative. In a positive sentence, it means "still" — the situation continues: まだ雨が降っている (it's still raining). In a negative sentence, it means "not yet" — the situation hasn't started: まだ食べていない (I haven't eaten yet). Same word, opposite time directions. "Still" looks backward at something ongoing. "Not yet" looks forward at something expected.

The most useful phrase with まだ might be the one-word answer: まだ。 Someone asks if you're done? まだ。 (Not yet.) Are they here? まだ。 (Not yet.) Have you decided? まだ。 It works as a complete sentence because the negative verb is implied. The question already established the action — まだ just tells you it hasn't happened.

まだ vs もう: These are a pair and they contrast on two axes. もう食べた = already ate. まだ食べていない = haven't eaten yet. もう食べない = won't eat anymore. まだ食べている = still eating. Think of もう as the word for change (something shifted) and まだ as the word for no change (the old state persists). They push against each other in every conversation about time and completion.

あと points backward and forward at the same time. It means "after" in the sense of what comes next (あとで電話する I'll call later), but also "remaining" in the sense of what's left (あと三つ three more to go). The connecting idea: what trails behind. The work left behind to finish. The time left behind to pass.

The kanji, when written, is 後 — the same character used in 午後 (ごご, afternoon) and 後ろ (うしろ, behind). But in everyday writing, あと often appears in hiragana, especially in its casual, conversational uses. Written as 後 it feels more formal; as あと it's breezy and spoken.

The "remaining" sense is the one you'll use constantly. あと五分 (five more minutes). あと少し (just a little more). あと何がいる？ (what else do you need?). It's a countdown word — it tells you the gap between where you are and where you're headed. When someone says あとちょっと, they mean the finish line is close.

聞く does three jobs that English splits across three separate verbs: hear, listen, and ask. The logic is this: all three involve receiving information through your ears. You hear a sound. You listen to music. You ask a question — and then you hear the answer. The kanji 聞 makes this explicit: 耳 (ear) tucked inside 門 (gate). An ear at the gate, straining to catch what's being said on the other side.

Which meaning? Context and particles sort it out. 音楽を聞く (listen to music) — you're actively taking sound in. 先生に聞く (ask the teacher) — you're directing your ear toward a person for an answer. 声が聞こえる uses the related word 聞こえる (to be audible) for involuntary hearing — sounds that reach you whether you want them or not. 聞く itself implies at least some intention.

There's a separate kanji, 聴く, that means "listen" with deep attention — as in 音楽を聴く for focused, appreciative listening. The difference is like English "hear" vs "listen" cranked up a notch. In casual writing, 聞く covers everything. 聴く is for when you want to signal that you're really paying attention.

Particles: 聞く takes を for the thing heard or listened to — 音楽を聞く. For "ask," the person asked takes に — 先生に聞く (ask the teacher). The question content can be marked with を or framed with と: 道を聞く (ask for directions).

Potential が聞ける: が marks what can be heard. ここで生の音楽が聞ける (you can hear live music here). The thing becomes the subject — it's available to be heard.

Passive に聞かれる: に marks who overhears or asks you. 隣の人に聞かれた (the person next to me overheard). Often carries a nuance of unwanted exposure.

Causative に…を聞かせる: に marks the listener, を marks what they hear. 子供に音楽を聞かせる (have the kid listen to music). 聞かせる also works as a standalone meaning "to let someone hear" — いい話を聞かせてよ (tell me something good).

五段 Godan他動詞
Dictionaryきくを
Negativeきかないを
Politeききますを
Polite negききませんを
Pastきいたを
Polite pastききましたを
て-formきいてを
Volitionalきこうを
Polite volききましょうを
Passiveきかれるに
Causativeきかせるに／を
Caus-passきかせられるに
Potentialきけるが
Imperativeきけを
Imp なさいききなさいを
Cond ～ばきけばを
Cond ～たらきいたらを

The kanji 言 is ancient and blunt: a mouth (口) with lines coming out of it. Sound waves, words, speech made visible. In oracle bone script it was even more literal — a tongue sticking out of a mouth with marks above it for the sounds. Every kanji that contains 言 as a radical relates to language: 話 (speak), 語 (language), 読 (read), 記 (record), 計 (count/measure). When you see 言 or its compressed form 訁 on the left side of a character, you know words are involved.

言う is the most basic speech verb. Not "speak" (that's 話す), not "tell" exactly (that's more 伝える) — just the act of producing words. 何と言った？ (what did you say?). The quotative particle と is its constant companion: 「行く」と言った (said "I'm going"). Almost every use of 言う has と right before it, framing the words that were said.

Hidden pattern: 〜という (sometimes written 〜と言う) is one of the most common constructions in Japanese. It means "called" or "that says" and it introduces or defines things: 「桜」という花 (a flower called "sakura"). 行くということ (the fact that [someone] is going). Once you start noticing という, you'll find it in every other sentence.

Particles: 言う takes と to frame what's said — 「はい」と言う. The person you say it to takes に — 友達に言う (say to a friend). Content can also take を — 文句を言う (say a complaint / complain).

Potential が言える: が marks what can be said. 日本語で名前が言える (I can say my name in Japanese).

Passive に言われる: に marks who said it. 先生にそう言われた (the teacher told me that). Very common — being told things by others is a frequent experience.

Causative に…を言わせる: に marks who you make speak, を marks what they say. 子供に「ごめんなさい」を言わせる (make the child say sorry). Also used idiomatically: 言わせてもらえば (if you'll let me say...).

五段 Godan他動詞
Dictionaryいうと
Negativeいわないと
Politeいいますと
Polite negいいませんと
Pastいったと
Polite pastいいましたと
て-formいってと
Volitionalいおうと
Polite volいいましょうと
Passiveいわれるに
Causativeいわせるに／を
Caus-passいわせられるに
Potentialいえるが
Imperativeいえと
Imp なさいいいなさいと
Cond ～ばいえばと
Cond ～たらいったらと

少ない is 多い's twin — and it shares the same grammatical curse. Just like 多い, you can't put 少ない directly before a noun. 少ない人 doesn't work. You need 少ない人数 or rephrase entirely: 人が少ない (people are few). These are the only two い-adjectives in the language with this restriction. Nobody has a satisfying explanation for why. They're just the rebels.

The kanji 少 is 小 (small) with an extra stroke — a slash cutting through it, as if paring something down even further. Small, then smaller. Not much left. The two readings split neatly: すくない as an adjective (few), and すこし as a noun/adverb (a little bit). Same kanji, two words. 時間が少ない (there's little time — adjective). 少し待って (wait a bit — adverb).

少ない vs 少し: Learners sometimes confuse these. 少ない describes a quantity that's insufficient or notably small — there's a judgment in it. 少し is neutral — just "a small amount." 経験が少ない means you have too little experience. 少し経験がある means you have a bit of experience. One implies a problem. The other doesn't.

Dictionaryすくない
Negativeすくなくない
Pastすくなかった
Past negすくなくなかった
Adverbすくなく
て-formすくなくて

高い means two things that seem unrelated in English — "tall" and "expensive" — but in Japanese the connection is obvious. Both point upward. A tall building reaches high. A high price is an elevated number. The kanji 高 is a picture of a tall building: a base, walls, and a peaked roof. The oldest forms looked like a watchtower or a multi-story structure. Height, in every sense.

Which meaning applies is always clear from context. 背が高い (tall — height of a person). 値段が高い (expensive — the price is high). 山が高い (the mountain is tall). この店は高い (this shop is expensive). You'll also hear it for figurative height: レベルが高い (high level), 評価が高い (highly rated).

Opposite pair: For height, the opposite is 低い (ひくい, low/short). For price, the opposite is 安い (やすい, cheap). Japanese doesn't mind that one adjective has two different antonyms — each meaning lives in its own lane.

Dictionaryたかい
Negativeたかくない
Pastたかかった
Past negたかくなかった
Adverbたかく
て-formたかくて

Two kanji: 子 (child) and 供 (offer, accompany). The etymology of the compound is a little dark: 供 originally meant "attendant" or "one who serves." 子供 may have started as "child-attendant" — children seen as little people who accompany adults. Some modern writers find this connotation uncomfortable and write 子ども (with hiragana ども) to soften it. You'll see both spellings in the wild.

The kanji 子 alone is a drawing of a baby — the oldest forms showed a swaddled infant with arms outstretched. It's one of the most versatile characters in the language. As a suffix, 子 turns up in girls' names (花子, 洋子), in words for small things (椅子 chair, 帽子 hat — neither of which has anything to do with children), and in scientific terms (原子 atom, 電子 electron).

子供 is the general, everyday word for "child/children." There's no singular/plural distinction — 子供 can be one kid or many kids. For your own child specifically, you might say うちの子 (my kid — using the intimate うち). For someone else's child politely: お子さん (your child — with the honorific お and さん). The register shift is automatic in Japanese: your own children are 子供, other people's are お子さん.

そう belongs to the ko-so-a-do system — it's the そ member, which means it refers to something near the listener or something just mentioned. そうです (that's right / that's so) is agreement — you're confirming what the other person just said. Their statement is in "their zone," so it gets そう.

This tiny word handles an enormous amount of conversational work. そうだ after a plain form means hearsay — "I heard that..." 雨が降るそうだ (I hear it's going to rain). そう after a verb stem means "looks like" — 降りそう (looks like it'll rain). Same sound, completely different grammar, and you tell them apart by what comes before そう. These are separate grammatical constructions that happen to use the same word — don't try to unify them. Just learn them as distinct patterns.

In conversation, そう is the universal back-channel response. Someone tells you something and you need to acknowledge it? そう。 そうですか。 そうなんだ。 Each has a slightly different flavor — from flat acknowledgment to mild surprise to genuine "oh, really?" — but they all run on the same そう engine.

もう is the word of change. Something shifted. The old state ended and a new one began — or is about to. That's the thread connecting all its seemingly different meanings: "already" (the change happened), "soon" (the change is imminent), "anymore" (in negative: the change means it's over), and even "another" (one more — adding to the count).

In positive sentences: もう食べた (already ate — it's done). もう帰る (I'm heading home now — the moment of departure has arrived). もうすぐ (soon — the change is close). In negative sentences: もう食べない (won't eat anymore — that's finished). もう会えない (can't meet anymore). The positive and negative uses feel opposite but the core is identical: a transition point has been reached.

Then there's the counting sense, which seems unrelated: もう一つ (one more). もう一回 (one more time). Here もう means "additionally" — you've already got some, and you're adding to the pile. It's still about movement past a previous state: you had one, now you have another.

And then there's the exclamation: もう！ — frustration, exasperation, "ugh, enough already!" It's もう at its most emotional — a declaration that you've hit your limit.

学 (study, learning) + 生 (life, person) = a person whose life is studying. The kanji 学 is worth examining: the top part (⺍) represents hands reaching for knowledge, the middle (冖) is a roof (a school building), and the bottom is 子 (a child). A child under a roof, hands grasping upward. That's education in a single character.

Word neighborhood: Japanese has multiple words for "student" and using the wrong one sounds odd. 学生 (がくせい) means a student at a university or higher institution — it's the default for college students. 生徒 (せいと) is for middle and high school students. 児童 (じどう) is for elementary school kids. And 学生 in casual speech can stretch to cover students in general, but in any formal context, the distinctions matter. Call a middle schooler a 学生 and a Japanese person will mentally correct you.

In daily life, 学生 also functions as an identity marker beyond academics. 学生割引 (がくせいわりびき, student discount). 学生時代 (がくせいじだい, one's student days) — a nostalgic reference to a specific period of life. Being a 学生 in Japan is a social role with its own rules, expectations, and freedoms. It's not just what you do. It's a chapter of your life with a definite start and end.

Japanese has two words pronounced あつい that both mean "hot" — and mixing them up is one of the classic beginner errors. 熱い is hot to the touch: hot coffee, a hot stove, a feverish forehead. 暑い is hot weather: a sweltering summer day, a stuffy room. The distinction is physical: 熱い is about an object's temperature. 暑い is about the air around you.

The kanji tells the story. 熱 has the fire radical (灬, four dots representing flames) at the bottom — fire heating something up, making an object hot. 暑 has 日 (sun) at the top — the sun beating down, making the environment hot. Fire from below vs. sun from above. Object heat vs. ambient heat.

熱い also carries a figurative meaning: passionate, intense. 熱い男 (a passionate man — someone who burns with intensity). 熱い試合 (an intense match). The metaphor is the same as English "burning passion" — but in Japanese it uses the touch-heat word, not the weather-heat word. Passion is something you feel radiating from a person, like heat from a surface.

Dictionaryあつい
Negativeあつくない
Pastあつかった
Past negあつくなかった
Adverbあつく
て-formあつくて

どうぞ is the gesture of an open hand — "here, take it," "go ahead," "after you." It's not the requesting kind of "please" (that's ください). It's the offering kind. You use it when you're giving permission, presenting something, or stepping aside for someone else.

どうぞ座ってください (please, have a seat). どうぞお入りください (please come in). どうぞ alone, with a gesture — handing someone a drink, holding a door open, offering a chair. The word carries warmth. It says: I want you to have this, do this, be comfortable. It's hospitality compressed into three syllables.

どうぞ vs ぜひ: Both get translated as "please" in some contexts, but they're different. どうぞ is offering or permitting — the ball is in the other person's court. ぜひ (by all means) is enthusiasm — you're urging someone to do something because you think they should: ぜひ来てください (you should definitely come). どうぞ opens a door. ぜひ pushes you through it.

午 means "noon" — specifically, the horse hour in the old Chinese zodiac time system, which corresponded to 11 AM–1 PM. 後 means "after." So 午後 is literally "after noon" — and English "afternoon" is the exact same construction. Two languages, same logic, built independently.

The zodiac connection goes deeper. 午 is the seventh of the twelve earthly branches, assigned to the horse (馬). That's why the word for noon in Japanese is 正午 (しょうご, exactly noon) — the precise middle of the horse hour. And the cardinal direction south is also associated with 午, because the sun is due south at noon. 子午線 (しごせん, the meridian line) literally runs from 子 (rat/north/midnight) to 午 (horse/south/noon).

In practice, 午後 goes before the time: 午後三時 (3 PM). Its pair is 午前 (ごぜん, before noon / AM). These are the formal time markers — you'll see them on clocks, schedules, and official documents. In conversation, people more often just use the 24-hour clock or add context.

The kanji 長 is one of those characters that started as a picture of a person. Specifically, a person with very long hair — in the oldest oracle bone inscriptions, you can see a figure with hair streaming down past their waist. Long hair was a mark of old age and wisdom in ancient China, which is why 長 also means "chief" or "elder": 社長 (しゃちょう, company president), 校長 (こうちょう, school principal), 部長 (ぶちょう, department head). The person with the longest hair has been around the longest.

長い covers length in space and time. 長い道 (a long road). 長い時間 (a long time). 話が長い (the talk is long — someone won't stop talking). Unlike English, where "long" and "tall" split, Japanese uses 長い for horizontal extension and 高い for vertical — a rope is 長い, a building is 高い.

Its opposite, 短い (みじかい, short), is worth noting here because they form one of the cleanest antonym pairs in Japanese. 長い for anything that extends. 短い for anything that doesn't extend enough.

Dictionaryながい
Negativeながくない
Pastながかった
Past negながくなかった
Adverbながく
て-formながくて

The kanji 本 is 木 (tree) with a horizontal line crossing the base of the trunk. That line marks the root — the foundation, the origin. 本 doesn't originally mean "book" at all. It means "root, origin, the real thing." Books came later, because books were considered the root of knowledge. The "origin" meaning lives on everywhere: 日本 (にほん, Japan — "origin of the sun"), 本当 (ほんとう, true — "original reality"), 本人 (ほんにん, the person themselves), 基本 (きほん, fundamentals).

As a counter, 本 counts long, thin objects: pencils, bottles, roads, rivers, telephone poles, movies (reels of film were long), phone calls (running through wires). ビール三本 (three bottles of beer). 映画を一本見た (watched one movie). The counter readings are irregular: いっぽん, にほん, さんぼん... The ほ shifts to ぽ or ぼ depending on the number before it.

For "book" specifically, 本 is the everyday word. 本を読む (read a book). 本屋 (ほんや, bookstore). 絵本 (えほん, picture book). Simple and ubiquitous.

The reading is the interesting part. 今 is normally いま or こん. 年 is normally ねん or とし. But 今年 is neither いまねん nor こんねん — it's ことし, a native Japanese word that got the Chinese characters pasted over it. こ is an archaic prefix meaning "this" (same こ as in ここ, これ) and とし is the native word for "year." The reading predates the kanji — it's ancient Japanese wearing a Chinese-character costume.

This is part of a family: 今日 (きょう, today), 今朝 (けさ, this morning), 今年 (ことし, this year) — all have irregular readings because they're old native words. Meanwhile, 今週 (こんしゅう, this week) and 今月 (こんげつ, this month) use regular Chinese-derived readings. The rule of thumb: the more frequently a time word is used in daily speech, the more likely it is to have kept its native Japanese reading.

今年こそ is a phrase you'll hear every January — "this year, for sure." The こそ adds emphasis, making it a declaration. New Year's resolutions, Japanese style.

よく is the adverb form of いい/よい (good) — and it kept both senses when it became an adverb. "Well" is straightforward: よくできた (well done). よく分かる (I understand well). But "often"? That seems like a leap — until you realize the logic. Doing something well implies doing it thoroughly, which implies doing it frequently. You know a place well because you go there often. The meanings bleed into each other.

Context sorts it out instantly. よく食べるね (you eat a lot / you eat often). よく聞こえる (I can hear well). よく来たね (good of you to come / I'm glad you came) — this one plays on both meanings at once.

There's also a sarcastic/exasperated よく: よくそんなことが言えるね (how can you say such a thing?). Here it means something like "you have the nerve to" — it's marveling at someone's audacity. The "well" meaning twisted into "how dare you do that so confidently." This usage is common in arguments and drama.

彼女 has a split personality. In formal or written Japanese, it means "she" — a third-person pronoun. In casual speech, it means "girlfriend." 彼女は来ますか in a business context means "is she coming?" At a bar with friends, 彼女いるの？ means "do you have a girlfriend?" The same word, but context flips the meaning entirely.

The kanji: 彼 (that person, he) + 女 (woman). Literally, "that woman." 彼 alone means "he" or "boyfriend" (same dual-meaning pattern). These words were actually coined in the Meiji era — Japan needed pronouns to translate Western novels, so scholars repurposed old demonstrative words. Before that, Japanese didn't really have third-person pronouns the way European languages do. People were referred to by name, title, or role.

This is important: native Japanese speakers use 彼女 and 彼 far less than English speakers use "she" and "he." In conversation, you refer to people by name — 田中さんは..., not 彼女は.... Overusing 彼女/彼 as pronouns is a tell that someone learned Japanese through translation rather than conversation. Use them when the context genuinely calls for them, not as default substitutes for people's names.

どう is the question member of the ko-so-a-do manner series: こう (like this), そう (like that), ああ (like that over there), どう (how?). It asks about the manner or state of something — not "what" (that's 何) but "in what way."

The phrase you'll use most: どうですか (how is it?). It's the Swiss army knife of Japanese questions. Asking about food at a restaurant? どうですか。 Someone's health? どうですか。 A proposal? どうですか。 It works for anything where you want someone's impression or assessment.

どう also appears in compounds that are so common they feel like single words. どうして (why — literally "doing what way"). どうも (thanks, somehow, very much — a vague intensifier that defies clean translation). どうぞ (please, go ahead). どうしても (no matter what, by all means). Master these どう compounds and you've unlocked a huge chunk of everyday conversational Japanese.

言 (say, speech) + 葉 (leaf). Words are leaves — they grow from the branches of thought and scatter in the wind. It's one of the most poetic kanji compounds in the language. Some scholars think the metaphor comes from the idea that words multiply and spread like leaves on a tree. Others point to the 万葉集 (まんようしゅう, Collection of Ten Thousand Leaves), Japan's oldest poetry anthology, which may have cemented the connection between language and foliage.

ことば is broader than English "word." A single word is a ことば. A phrase is a ことば. An entire language is ことば. Even the way someone speaks — their manner of expression — is their ことば. 言葉が見つからない (I can't find the words). 言葉遣い (ことばづかい, word usage / how someone talks). 日本語という言葉 (the language called Japanese).

There's a famous Japanese proverb: 言葉は心の使い — "words are the messengers of the heart." In a culture that values reading between the lines and understanding what's not said, 言葉 carries extra weight. The words someone chooses reveal as much as the words themselves.

The kanji 顔 has 頁 (head) on the right — that component shows up in every head-related kanji (頭 head, 額 forehead, 顎 jaw). The left side, 彦, was originally a man of fine appearance. Together: the part of the head that shows your character. In Japanese culture, 顔 isn't just anatomy — it's identity, reputation, and social standing.

顔が広い (having a wide face — knowing lots of people, being well-connected). 顔を出す (to show one's face — to make an appearance). 顔を立てる (to preserve someone's face — to protect their dignity). 顔が利く (one's face works — having influence). The concept of "face" as social reputation runs deep in Japanese, just as it does in Chinese — and the word 顔 carries all of that weight alongside its literal meaning of the physical face.

For the physical face in a medical or objective sense, Japanese sometimes uses 顔面 (がんめん, face-surface). But in everyday speech, 顔 handles everything from "wash your face" (顔を洗う) to "she has a familiar face" (見覚えのある顔). It's one of those body-part words that carries an enormous amount of cultural and social meaning beyond the anatomy.

The kanji 終 is 糸 (thread) + 冬 (winter). The end of the thread. Winter — the end of the growing season. Everything about this character points to a stopping place. The thread runs out. The warmth fades. Things come to their conclusion.

終わる is intransitive — things end on their own. 授業が終わる (class ends). 映画が終わった (the movie ended). Its transitive partner is 終える (おえる) — you finish something, actively bringing it to a close: 仕事を終える (finish the work). The distinction: 終わる describes the event of ending. 終える describes the act of ending something.

Key pattern: 〜終わる attached to a verb's masu-stem means "finish doing." 読み終わる (finish reading). 食べ終わる (finish eating). It's one of the most useful compound verb patterns — it tells you not just that something ended, but that you completed the action to its natural conclusion.

Particles: 終わる takes が — the thing that ends is the subject. 会議が終わる (the meeting ends). Since it's intransitive, there's no direct object — things end by themselves.

Passive に終わられる: rare, but used for indirect passive — when something ending negatively affects you. 先に終わられた (they finished before me — and it put me at a disadvantage).

Causative を／に終わらせる: making something end. を marks what you're ending — 会議を終わらせる (end the meeting). This causative form essentially works like the transitive version.

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 終える (おえる)
Dictionaryおわるが
Negativeおわらないが
Politeおわりますが
Polite negおわりませんが
Pastおわったが
Polite pastおわりましたが
て-formおわってが
Volitionalおわろうが
Polite volおわりましょうが
Passiveおわられるに
Causativeおわらせるを／に
Caus-passおわらせられるに
Potentialおわれるが
Imperativeおわれが
Imp なさいおわりなさいが
Cond ～ばおわればが
Cond ～たらおわったらが

Japanese has two number systems running in parallel. The Chinese-derived system (いち、に、さん...) and the native Japanese system (ひとつ、ふたつ、みっつ...). ひとつ is the native system's "one" — and it's the system you use when counting generic things without a specific counter. Don't know what counter to use? ひとつ works. It's the safe default.

The native counting system only goes to ten (とお) and then stops. After that, everything switches to Chinese numbers. This tells you something about its age — it predates extensive Chinese linguistic influence, and it was probably sufficient for daily life in an era before large-scale accounting. You'll encounter all ten (ひとつ through とお) regularly, so they're worth memorizing as a set.

一つ also works as "one" in the sense of an option or a point: 一つ聞いてもいい？ (can I ask you one thing?). 一つ問題がある (there's one problem). And it shows up in the expression 一つずつ (one by one), where ずつ means "each" — a useful pattern that works with any number.

Japanese has three verbs for "give" and they're not interchangeable — they encode the social direction of the gift. あげる means giving away from the speaker: I give to you, I give to someone, they give to someone. くれる means giving toward the speaker: you give to me, someone gives to me. もらう means receiving. Get these mixed up and your sentence will sound like the social gravity is backwards.

あげる literally means "to raise" — the same word as lifting something up, 手を上げる (raise your hand). The giving sense may come from the gesture of raising something up to offer it to someone. Or from the idea of elevating — putting the other person above you. Either way, あげる inherently positions the receiver as higher.

Key pattern: 〜てあげる means doing something as a favor for someone. 教えてあげる (I'll teach you — as a favor). 手伝ってあげた (I helped them — implying generosity). Be careful: this pattern can sound condescending if used wrong. 説明してあげる can come across as "I'll graciously explain it to you" if the context doesn't warrant it. Use it when the favor is real.

Particles: あげる takes に for the receiver and を for the thing given. 友達にプレゼントをあげる (give a present to a friend). に marks the destination — where the gift ends up.

Potential があげられる: が marks what can be given. いいアドバイスがあげられる (I can give good advice).

Passive にあげられる: rarely used — giving is inherently active, not something done to you. You'd more naturally use もらう to express the receiving side.

Causative にあげさせる: に marks who you make give. 子供にお菓子をあげさせる (make the child give out candy).

一段 Ichidan他動詞
Dictionaryあげるを
Negativeあげないを
Politeあげますを
Polite negあげませんを
Pastあげたを
Polite pastあげましたを
て-formあげてを
Volitionalあげようを
Polite volあげましょうを
Passiveあげられるに
Causativeあげさせるに／を
Caus-passあげさせられるに
Potentialあげられるが
Imperativeあげろを
Imp なさいあげなさいを
Cond ～ばあげればを
Cond ～たらあげたらを

こう is the こ member of the manner series: こう (like this — my way), そう (like that — your way), ああ (like that — distant), どう (how?). Where これ points at a thing near you, こう points at a manner or method near you — something you're about to demonstrate or have just shown.

It almost always comes with a demonstration. こうやって開ける (you open it like this) — and then you show them. こう書く (you write it like this) — and you write it. こう is the word you say while your hands are moving, while you're physically showing someone the way to do something. It's deictic — it points at something present in the shared moment.

In storytelling, こう introduces direct speech or thought: 彼はこう言った (he said the following). It sets up what's about to come — "here's what it was like." And in arguments or reasoning: こう考えてみて (think about it this way) — you're about to reframe the situation from your angle.

学 (study) + 校 (school building, proofreading). The second kanji is interesting: 校 has the tree radical (木) on the left and 交 (cross, exchange) on the right. The original meaning was a wooden frame used for binding or checking things — which evolved into both "a place of structured learning" and "to proofread" (校正, こうせい). A school is where knowledge gets structured. Proofreading is where text gets checked. Both meanings come from the same idea of putting things in order.

The pronunciation has a small trap: がっこう, not がくこう. The く of 学 assimilates into a double consonant before the こ of 校. This kind of sound change (called 促音, そくおん) happens regularly when certain syllables bump up against voiceless consonants. You'll see the same thing in 六階 → ろっかい and 一回 → いっかい.

学校 is the general word for school — elementary through high school. For university, it's 大学. For a specific type of school, you stack descriptors: 小学校 (しょうがっこう, elementary school), 中学校 (ちゅうがっこう, middle school), 高校 (こうこう, high school — shortened from 高等学校).

くれる is giving seen from the receiver's side — and specifically, the receiver is you (or your in-group). This is the verb that makes Japanese giving/receiving unique: the language forces you to encode who benefits. あげる sends gifts outward. くれる receives them inward. 友達がプレゼントをくれた (my friend gave me a present). The friend is the subject, but the emotional center is the speaker — someone did something nice for me.

The crucial pattern: 〜てくれる means someone does something as a favor for you. 教えてくれた (they taught me — and I'm grateful). 手伝ってくれる？ (will you help me?). This is warmer and more personal than a neutral request. Using 〜てくれる acknowledges that the other person's action benefits you — there's built-in gratitude. Compare 手伝ってもらえる？ (more about the outcome) with 手伝ってくれる？ (more about the person helping). Both are requests, but くれる feels more direct and personal.

くれる vs もらう: Both describe someone doing you a favor, but from different angles. 友達が教えてくれた (friend taught me — friend is the subject, friend is generous). 友達に教えてもらった (I got taught by my friend — I am the subject, I received the benefit). Same event, different framing. Japanese uses both constantly.

Particles: くれる takes を for the thing given. The giver is the subject (marked by が). 母が本をくれた (mom gave me a book). The receiver (you) is usually omitted because it's implied.

Potential がくれられる: almost never used. If you want to say someone "can give," rephrase with もらえる — "I can receive."

Passive: not used — くれる already encodes the receiver's perspective, making passive redundant.

Causative: not naturally used. The giving/receiving system doesn't layer with causative in practice.

The kanji 始 is 女 (woman) + 台 (platform, base). The connection to "begin" traces back to the idea of a woman as the origin of life — birth as the first beginning. The same kanji appears in 原始 (げんし, primitive/primordial) and 始祖 (しそ, founder/ancestor), always carrying that sense of original starting point.

始める is transitive — you start something. 仕事を始める (start work). 勉強を始める (start studying). Its intransitive partner is 始まる (はじまる) — something starts on its own: 映画が始まる (the movie starts). This is a classic える/ある transitive/intransitive pair, and you'll see the pattern again and again: 終える/終わる, 変える/変わる, 決める/決まる.

Key pattern: 〜始める attached to a verb's masu-stem means "start doing." 雨が降り始めた (it started raining). 読み始める (start reading). This is the mirror image of 〜終わる (finish doing). Together they bracket any action.

Particles: 始める takes を — the thing you start is your direct object. 新しい生活を始める (start a new life).

Potential が始められる: が marks what can be started. 来月から授業が始められる (classes can be started from next month).

Passive に始められる: に marks who started it. 先に始められた (they started before me).

Causative に始めさせる: に marks who you make start. 生徒に練習を始めさせる (have the students start practice).

meaning: to wake, get up; to occur
lesson:
The kanji 起 is 走 (run) + 己 (self). The self, running — rising up and getting moving. That captures both meanings: physically waking up (your body rises), and events occurring (things rise into being). The motion is always upward, from horizontal to vertical, from dormant to active.

朝早く起きる (wake up early in the morning). 事故が起きた (an accident occurred). 地震が起きる (an earthquake happens). The "wake up" meaning is for people. The "occur" meaning is for events, problems, phenomena. You won't confuse them because the subject tells you which is which — a person wakes up, a situation arises.

起きる vs 起こる vs 起こす: Three related verbs. 起きる is intransitive — you wake up, things happen. 起こる (おこる) is also intransitive but only means "occur" — it doesn't mean "wake up." 起こす (おこす) is transitive — you wake someone up, you cause something to happen. 子供を起こす (wake the kid up). 問題を起こす (cause a problem).

Particles: 起きる takes が for the subject — the person who wakes or the event that occurs. 子供が起きた (the kid woke up). No direct object — it's intransitive. For time, use に: 六時に起きる (wake up at six).

Passive: not commonly used — waking up is something you do, not something done to you. For events, you'd say 起こされた (the transitive causative) if someone caused it.

Causative を／に起きさせる: making someone wake up. 子供を起きさせる (make the kid get up). In practice, 起こす (the transitive pair) is more natural for "wake someone up."

The kanji 春 has 日 (sun) at the bottom and three strokes on top that originally represented emerging plants. Sun warming the earth, making things push upward — spring. In the oldest Chinese script, the character was more explicitly a picture of seedlings sprouting under sunlight. What survived is still legible if you squint: the energy of growth rising toward warmth.

はる is deeply woven into Japanese culture in a way that no other season quite matches. 春 is cherry blossom season, the start of the school year, the fiscal year, new jobs, fresh starts. While Western new beginnings cluster around January, Japanese ones cluster around April. 春休み (はるやすみ, spring break) marks the boundary between school years — it's not a vacation in the middle of the year, it's the transition between endings and new beginnings.

The word はる may be related to the verb 張る (はる, to swell/stretch) — the season when buds swell and burst open. Some linguists also connect it to 墾る (はる, to plow/clear land), since spring is when farmers begin working the fields. Either etymology roots the word in physical expansion — things opening up after winter's constriction.

午前 is the mirror of 午後: 午 (noon) + 前 (before) = "before noon." Together with 午後, they split the day at its midpoint. Clean, symmetrical, borrowed wholesale from the Chinese system of dividing time by the zodiac hours.

In usage, 午前 goes before the time: 午前十時 (10 AM). But here's something worth noticing: Japanese people don't always say 午前 for "morning" in casual speech. They're more likely to say 朝 (あさ) for early morning or just use context. 午前 is the precise, clock-oriented word — schedules, appointments, news broadcasts. 朝 is the lived experience of morning — waking up, the commute, breakfast.

午前中 (ごぜんちゅう) means "during the morning" or "throughout the morning" and is extremely useful: 午前中に届きます (it'll arrive sometime in the morning). The 中 adds a sense of "somewhere within that span" — no exact time, just the general morning window.

The kanji 別 has a blade (刂, the knife radical) on the right — cutting something apart, separating. The left side (另) originally depicted a mouth and a bone, suggesting the act of cutting flesh from bone. Separation at its most visceral. This kanji shows up in 別れる (わかれる, to part/break up), 区別 (くべつ, distinction), 特別 (とくべつ, special — literally "especially separate").

As a な-adjective, 別 means "separate" or "different" and goes before nouns with な: 別な問題 (a different problem). But it also frequently appears without な, acting more like a noun: 別の話 (a different topic). Both 別な and 別の are correct and common — 別の is slightly more frequent in modern speech.

別に as an adverb is one of those words you'll hear constantly in casual Japanese. 別にいい (I don't really care / it's fine). 別に何もない (nothing in particular). It carries a dismissive, slightly indifferent tone — "not especially," "not particularly." In anime and drama, 別に... followed by a trailing-off is the classic tsundere response: pretending not to care.

Dictionaryべつだ
Negativeべつじゃない
Pastべつだった
Past negべつじゃなかった
Adverbべつに
て-formべつで

どこ is the question member of the ko-so-a-do place series: ここ (here), そこ (there, near you), あそこ (over there), どこ (where?). The system is perfectly regular — swap the first syllable and the meaning changes. You already know the logic from これ/それ/あれ/どれ.

どこに行くの？ (where are you going?). トイレはどこですか (where's the bathroom?). どこから来ましたか (where are you from?). The particle after どこ shapes the question: に asks where you're heading, で asks where something happens, から asks where something comes from.

Like all question words in Japanese, どこ shapeshifts with particles: どこか (somewhere), どこも (everywhere / nowhere, depending on positive or negative), どこでも (anywhere). The pattern is identical to いつか/いつも/いつでも — learn one question word's particle combinations and you've learned them all.

Two kanji: 部 (section, department) + 屋 (roof, shop, house). A sectioned-off space under a roof — a room. The reading へや is irregular; you'd expect ぶや from the standard readings, but this compound took its own path. Just memorize it as a unit.

屋 on its own is worth knowing — it appears everywhere. When it means "roof/building," it's read や: 本屋 (ほんや, bookstore), 花屋 (はなや, florist), パン屋 (bakery). It turns any product into the shop that sells it. The kanji itself shows a corpse radical (尸) — originally a person lying down — over a reaching gesture, representing a person sheltering under a roof. A body at rest under cover.

部屋 is the default word for any room in a building — your bedroom, a hotel room, a meeting room. 部屋に入る (enter a room). 部屋を片付ける (tidy up the room). In sumo, 部屋 also means a wrestling stable — the place where wrestlers live and train together. 相撲部屋 (すもうべや) is a compound you'll encounter if you follow sumo at all.

The kanji 若 has 艹 (grass radical) on top — young shoots, tender growth. The bottom part 右 was borrowed for its phonetic value, but the visual tells the story: new green growth. Something fresh, not yet mature. In Buddhist terminology, 般若 (はんにゃ) uses 若 for a completely different meaning (wisdom), a reminder that many kanji have wildly different lives in different contexts.

若い usually describes people, but it stretches to anything that seems fresh or unripe. 若い人 (a young person). 若い頃 (when I was young). まだ若い (still young). You can also hear 若く見える (look young) — a compliment in any culture, but especially effective in Japan.

Compounds: 若者 (わかもの, young people) is a common noun — you'll see it in news articles about generational trends. 若手 (わかて, a young/junior person) is used in workplaces and professions to describe someone still rising. Both treat youth not as an age but as a stage — not yet fully developed, still proving yourself.

Dictionaryわかい
Negativeわかくない
Pastわかかった
Past negわかくなかった
Adverbわかく
て-formわかくて

The kanji 車 is a top-down view of a cart: the axle running through the middle, two wheels on either side. This is one of the most faithful kanji-to-picture correspondences that survived from ancient script. Oracle bone versions from 3,500 years ago show a two-wheeled chariot so clearly you could build one from the drawing. The modern character is a simplification, but the bones are there — a central shaft with horizontal elements crossing it.

Originally 車 meant any wheeled vehicle — carts, carriages, rickshaws. In modern Japanese, it defaults to "car" in most contexts. 車で行く (go by car). 車を運転する (drive a car). But the older meaning survives in compounds: 電車 (でんしゃ, electric train), 自転車 (じてんしゃ, bicycle — literally "self-rotating vehicle"), 風車 (ふうしゃ, windmill). Any machine with wheels or rotating parts can claim 車.

The word くるま itself likely comes from くる (to spin) + ま (round thing) — the spinning round thing. A wheel. Then the thing with wheels. Then the car.

The kanji 置 has 网 (net) on top and 直 (straight, direct) on the bottom. The idea: setting a net down in a fixed position — placing something deliberately. 置く isn't about tossing something down casually. It implies intention — you're putting something where it belongs, or at least where you want it.

テーブルに置く (put on the table). 本棚に置く (place on the bookshelf). 鍵をどこに置いた？ (where did I put the keys?). The particle に marks the location — where the thing ends up — and を marks the thing being placed.

Grammar pattern: 〜ておく (often contracted to 〜とく in casual speech) means to do something in advance, as preparation. 買っておく (buy in advance). 調べておく (look it up beforehand). そのままにしておく (leave it as it is — a deliberate decision to not change something). The image is placing the action down for later use — doing it now so it's ready when you need it. This is one of the most useful grammar patterns in spoken Japanese.

Particles: 置く takes を for the thing placed and に for the location. 荷物をここに置く (put the luggage here).

Potential が置ける: が marks what can be placed. ここに車が置ける (you can park a car here).

Passive に置かれる: に marks who placed it. 変な場所に置かれた (it was placed in a weird spot).

Causative に…を置かせる: に marks who you make put something down. 子供に荷物を置かせる (have the kid put down the bags).

The kanji 住 is 亻 (person) + 主 (master, main). A person who is the master of a place — someone settled, rooted. Not passing through. 住む means to live somewhere as your base, your home. It's not about being alive (that's 生きる) and it's not about temporarily staying (that's 泊まる). It's about where your life is anchored.

Almost always used with に for the place and ている for the ongoing state: 東京に住んでいる (I live in Tokyo). Like 知る, the dictionary form 住む describes the act of settling somewhere, while 住んでいる describes the state of living there. You'll almost always want the ている form.

住 feeds into useful compounds: 住所 (じゅうしょ, address), 住民 (じゅうみん, resident), 住宅 (じゅうたく, residence/housing). The on'yomi じゅう appears in all formal/administrative contexts. The kun'yomi すむ stays in everyday speech.

Particles: 住む takes に — the place where you live. 日本に住む (live in Japan). に marks the location of residence, the place you're rooted to.

Passive に住まれる: uncommon, but possible in indirect passive — 隣に変な人に住まれて困っている (a weird person lives next door and it's a problem for me).

Causative を／に住ませる: making or letting someone live somewhere. に for permissive — 学生にこのアパートに住ませる (let students live in this apartment).

五段 Godan自動詞

働 is rare among kanji — it was invented in Japan, not imported from China. It's a 国字 (こくじ, national character), combining 亻 (person) + 動 (move). A person in motion. That's the Japanese definition of work: a human being moving, doing, physically engaging with the world. The Chinese character for work (工) is abstract — a beam or tool. The Japanese invention is embodied — it's about the worker, not the work.

働く is intransitive — you work, but you don't "work" an object. The place where you work takes で: 会社で働く (work at a company). The field takes で too: IT業界で働いている (I work in the IT industry). Like 住む, you'll most often use the ている form to describe your ongoing work situation.

働く can also mean "to function" or "to operate" for non-humans: 頭が働かない (my brain isn't working). 薬が働く (the medicine takes effect). And in a darker sense: 悪事を働く (commit a crime) — here 働く unusually takes を, treating the wrongdoing as the object of labor.

Particles: 働く takes で for the workplace — 病院で働く (work at a hospital). で marks where the action happens. For the company or organization specifically, に also works: トヨタに働いている.

Passive に働かれる: indirect passive — someone working affects you. Rare in natural speech.

Causative を／に働かせる: making someone work. を for coercive — 社員を休みなく働かせる (make employees work without days off). A word you see in discussions about labor rights.

五段 Godan自動詞
Dictionaryはたらくで

Politeはたらきますで
Polite negはたらきませんで
Pastはたらいたで
Polite pastはたらきましたで

Passiveはたらかれるに
Causativeはたらかせるを／に
Caus-passはたらかせられるに

Cond ～たらはたらいたらで

The kanji 難 is dense — 隹 (short-tailed bird) on the right and a complex left component that originally depicted a clay vessel near fire. The ancient meaning was "a bird that's hard to catch" or more broadly, "hardship, disaster." The character still carries both senses: 難しい (difficult) and 災難 (さいなん, disaster). Difficulty and suffering share a kanji because the ancient Chinese saw them as the same thing.

むずかしい describes intellectual or situational difficulty — a hard test, a complex problem, a tricky person. 日本語は難しい (Japanese is hard). 難しい顔をする (to make a difficult face — to look troubled or stern). That last expression is revealing: 難しい can describe people who are hard to deal with — picky, demanding, or just socially complicated.

難しい vs 大変: Both can mean "hard" but from different angles. 難しい is about complexity — a puzzle, a skill, a concept. 大変 (たいへん) is about the scale of effort or trouble — an overwhelming workload, a terrible situation. A math problem is 難しい. Moving house while working full-time is 大変. One challenges your brain. The other exhausts your everything.

Dictionaryむずかしい
Negativeむずかしくない
Pastむずかしかった
Past negむずかしくなかった
Adverbむずかしく
て-formむずかしくて

先 (before, ahead) + 生 (born, life) = someone born before you, someone who came first. That's the literal meaning, and it explains why 先生 extends far beyond classroom teachers. Doctors are 先生. Lawyers are 先生. Authors, politicians, martial arts masters — all 先生. The word honors anyone who has knowledge or authority that came from getting there first.

In practice, 先生 functions both as a noun ("a teacher") and as a title ("Doctor Tanaka" = 田中先生). When used as a title, it replaces さん — you don't say 田中先生さん. And you can address someone directly as just 先生, the way English uses "Doctor" or "Professor" standalone.

There's an ironic usage too. Sarcastically calling someone 先生 — especially in the third person — implies they're being arrogant or self-important. あの先生は偉そうだね can mean the person acts like they know everything. The same word that conveys deep respect can, with the right tone, convey the opposite.

The kanji 立 is a person standing on the ground. Two legs on a horizontal line. It's been this picture for over three thousand years — one of the most stable, recognizable kanji across the entire history of the writing system. A human being on their feet, vertical, present.

立つ is intransitive — you stand up, you rise, something stands. ここに立って (stand here). 椅子から立つ (stand up from a chair). Its transitive partner is 立てる (たてる) — to set something upright, to establish, to formulate: 計画を立てる (make a plan — stand it up). 旗を立てる (raise a flag).

立つ has a rich idiomatic life. 役に立つ (to be useful — to stand in service). 腹が立つ (to get angry — your belly rises). 目立つ (to stand out — the eyes stand, drawn to something). 立場 (たちば, standpoint/position). The vertical image runs through all of them — being upright means being visible, active, and present.

Particles: 立つ takes に for where you stand — 入口に立つ (stand at the entrance). For rising from somewhere, から: 席から立つ (stand up from your seat).

Passive に立たれる: indirect passive — someone standing somewhere affects you. 前に立たれて見えない (someone stood in front of me and I can't see).

Causative を／に立たせる: making someone stand. を for coercive — 生徒を廊下に立たせる (make a student stand in the hallway — a classic Japanese school punishment).

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 立てる (たてる)

The kanji 呼 has 口 (mouth) on the left and 乎 (an exclamation) on the right. A mouth crying out — calling to someone. The character's original sense was exhaling forcefully, shouting, summoning. It's the opposite of 吸 (すう, to inhale/suck in), and together they form 呼吸 (こきゅう, breathing — out and in).

呼ぶ covers both "to call out to someone" and "to call someone something." 名前を呼ぶ (call someone's name). タクシーを呼ぶ (call a taxi). 友達を呼ぶ (invite friends over — call them to come). 彼を「先生」と呼ぶ (call him "sensei"). The particle と frames the name or title, just like with 言う.

Notice that 呼ぶ can mean "invite" — calling someone to a place implies you want them there. パーティーに呼ぶ (invite to a party). The word doesn't draw a hard line between summoning and inviting. Whether it's a command or a welcome depends entirely on the relationship and context.

Particles: 呼ぶ takes を for the person called and と for what you call them. 彼を「田中さん」と呼ぶ (call him "Tanaka-san").

Potential が呼べる: が marks who can be called. ここからタクシーが呼べる (you can call a taxi from here).

Passive に呼ばれる: に marks who calls you. 先生に呼ばれた (I was called by the teacher). Very common — being summoned is a frequent experience.

Causative に…を呼ばせる: に marks who you make do the calling. 子供に友達を呼ばせる (have the kid call their friends).

大 (big) + 学 (study) = big study. Grand learning. In the Confucian tradition, 大学 was one of the Four Books — a foundational text about self-cultivation and governance. The word carried philosophical weight long before it meant an institution with a campus and tuition. A 大学 was supposed to be where you pursued the "great learning" — not just skills, but wisdom.

In modern Japan, 大学 is where the real social sorting happens. Which university you attend — 東大 (とうだい, University of Tokyo), 京大 (きょうだい, Kyoto University), 早稲田, 慶応 — often matters more for your career than what you study there. 大学に入る (enter university) is one of the defining moments of a Japanese person's life, preceded by the infamous 受験 (じゅけん, entrance exam) period.

Related words: 大学生 (だいがくせい, university student). 大学院 (だいがくいん, graduate school). 短大 (たんだい, junior college — short for 短期大学). The abbreviation pattern is common: long compound → first kanji of each part.

The kanji 安 is a woman (女) under a roof (宀). The original meaning wasn't "cheap" — it was "peace, safety." A woman safe inside a house. (Whether this etymology is charmingly protective or uncomfortably patriarchal depends on your perspective.) The "cheap" meaning came later, as an extension: peaceful → settled → easy → easy on the wallet.

That original "peaceful" sense still lives: 安心 (あんしん, peace of mind). 安全 (あんぜん, safety). 不安 (ふあん, anxiety — not-peace). When you see 安 in compounds with the on'yomi あん, it usually means safety or ease. When you hear it as やすい, it means cheap.

Suffix alert: 〜やすい attached to a verb's masu-stem means "easy to do" — and it's one of the most useful suffixes in the language. 読みやすい (easy to read). 分かりやすい (easy to understand). 壊れやすい (easy to break, fragile). This やすい is written in hiragana and is technically a different word from the adjective 安い, but the sound-meaning connection is real: easy, not burdensome, low-effort.

Dictionaryやすい
Negativeやすくない
Pastやすかった
Past negやすくなかった
Adverbやすく
て-formやすくて

もっと is the everyday, colloquial "more" — the one you reach for in conversation. もっと食べて (eat more). もっと大きい (bigger). もっと早く言って (you should have said so sooner — literally, say it more early). It intensifies whatever follows — more of the same quality, action, or degree.

Japanese doesn't have comparative adjective forms the way English does (no "-er" suffix). Instead, you just put もっと in front of the adjective: もっと安い (cheaper). もっと難しい (harder). Clean and consistent — no irregular comparatives, no "good/better/best" nightmares. Just もっと + any adjective.

もっと vs もう: もっと asks for a greater degree of the same thing. もう (+ a number/quantity) asks for an additional unit. もっと水 = more water (a larger amount). もう一杯 = another glass (one more of the same). The difference matters: もっと adjusts the dial. もう adds to the count.

The kanji 帰 has a complex history — the left component relates to a marching army, and the right (帚) is a broom. The original meaning was soldiers returning home, brooms sweeping the path for their arrival. Over time it simplified to just "return." But not any return — 帰る specifically means returning to where you belong. Home. Your country. Your base. The place where you're from.

This is the key distinction: 帰る vs 戻る (もどる). Both mean "return" but 帰る points homeward — the destination is your home base. 戻る means to go back to a previous location or state — any location. 家に帰る (go home). 席に戻る (return to your seat). You wouldn't say 席に帰る because your seat isn't "home."

帰りたい — "I want to go home" — might be the most relatable sentence in any language. In Japanese workplaces, announcing お先に失礼します (excuse me for leaving before you) is the ritual phrase for going home, because leaving before your colleagues requires an apology. 帰る is loaded with social weight in work culture.

Particles: 帰る takes に for the destination — 家に帰る (go home). It also takes から for where you're returning from: 学校から帰る (come home from school).

Passive に帰られる: indirect passive — someone going home affects you. 彼女に帰られた (she went home — and I was left behind / it affected me negatively). The に marks the person whose departure impacted you.

Causative を／に帰らせる: making or letting someone go home. を for coercive — 生徒を帰らせる (send the student home). に for permissive — 社員に早く帰らせる (let the employee go home early).
